title: The Bhagavad Gita
author: Attributed to Vyasa
narrativeFrame: Dialogue between Prince Arjuna and Lord Krishna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra

notableTranslations.notes: Most popular English translation; includes introduction and commentary
Critical edition with Sanskrit text, word-by-word translation, and detailed notes

historicalContext.setting: Battlefield of Kurukshetra, between the Pandava and Kaurava armies
historicalContext.period: Composed during the consolidation of Hinduism, post-Buddhism and Jainism
historicalContext.authorship: Traditionally attributed to sage Vyasa; scholars consider it a composite work by multiple authors
historicalContext.dating: Generally dated to 2nd-1st century BCE, with estimates ranging from 5th century BCE to 1st century CE

india: Adi Shankara's commentary (8th century CE) popularized the Gita
Central text in Vedanta and Vaishnava traditions
Inspired the Bhakti movement across India
Influenced Mahatma Gandhi's philosophy of nonviolent action
Swami Vivekananda's interpretations shaped modern Hinduism
west: Henry David Thoreau referenced it in Walden (1854)
Ralph Waldo Emerson was deeply influenced
Aldous Huxley cited it as inspiration for 'The Perennial Philosophy'
Robert Oppenheimer quoted it after the first atomic test
T.S. Eliot referenced it in 'The Dry Salvages'
